clinicalStem: A 28-year-old male presents to a rural health centre after 6 hours of profuse, painless, rice-water diarrhoea and vomiting following a community feast. On examination: pulse 124 bpm, BP 80/50 mmHg, eyes sunken, skin turgor markedly reduced, he is drowsy. He cannot drink without vomiting. Stool culture is pending. Which is the single most important immediate management step?
options: Start oral rehydration solution (ORS) and observe for 2 hours
Administer IV Ringer's lactate rapidly to restore circulation
Give oral doxycycline 300 mg stat and observe
Arrange urgent stool microscopy before any fluids
Give intramuscular metoclopramide and retry oral fluids
explanation: This patient is in hypovolaemic shock (BP 80/50, tachycardia, drowsiness) from severe cholera dehydration. WHO guidelines mandate rapid IV rehydration with Ringer's lactate (100 ml/kg over 3 hours for adults) as the immediate priority when the patient cannot tolerate oral fluids and is in shock. Antibiotics reduce duration and severity but circulatory resuscitation takes absolute precedence.
wrongAnswerExplanations: ORS is appropriate for mild-moderate dehydration, but this patient is in shock and cannot drink without vomiting — oral fluids alone are insufficient and dangerous here.
Correct. Rapid IV Ringer's lactate is the WHO-recommended first step for severe cholera with shock.
Doxycycline is a useful adjunct antibiotic but does nothing to correct the immediate life-threatening hypovolaemia; antibiotics alone without fluid resuscitation will not save this patient.
Investigations should not delay emergency resuscitation; cholera is a clinical diagnosis in a compatible epidemic/outbreak setting.
An antiemetic may help later, but managing circulatory collapse with IV fluids is the immediate priority — delaying for an antiemetic is dangerous.
clinicalPearl: In severe cholera with shock, IV Ringer's lactate resuscitation always precedes antibiotics — fluids save the life, antibiotics reduce duration.
keyClue: BP 80/50, drowsy, cannot tolerate oral fluids — hypovolaemic shock requiring immediate IV resuscitation.
commonExamTrap: Choosing doxycycline first because cholera is a bacterial infection, neglecting the immediate haemodynamic emergency.
missedConcept: Triage of dehydration severity and recognition that IV fluids, not antibiotics, are the emergency priority in cholera shock.
redFlagReason: Patient is in hypovolaemic shock (BP 80/50, drowsy) — delayed fluid resuscitation is immediately life-threatening.

clinicalStem: A 35-year-old woman presents with 12 hours of profuse watery diarrhoea and vomiting during a confirmed cholera outbreak in her district. She is alert, able to drink, and has moderate dehydration (sunken eyes, drinks eagerly, mild tachycardia). She is started on oral rehydration solution. The primary care physician decides to add an antibiotic to shorten illness duration. Which antibiotic is the most appropriate first-line choice for uncomplicated cholera in a non-pregnant adult?
options: Amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily for 5 days
Ciprofloxacin 1 g as a single oral dose
Metronidazole 400 mg three times daily for 7 days
Co-trimoxazole 960 mg twice daily for 5 days
Erythromycin 500 mg four times daily for 3 days
explanation: WHO guidelines recommend a single oral dose of ciprofloxacin (1 g for adults) as the preferred antibiotic adjunct for cholera in non-pregnant adults. It shortens the duration of diarrhoea and bacterial shedding. Single-dose therapy improves adherence in resource-limited settings.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Amoxicillin has no proven efficacy against Vibrio cholerae and is not recommended for cholera management.
Correct. Single-dose ciprofloxacin 1 g is the WHO first-line antibiotic for non-pregnant adults with cholera.
Metronidazole targets anaerobes and protozoa; Vibrio cholerae is a Gram-negative aerobe and metronidazole has no role here.
Co-trimoxazole was previously used but widespread resistance has made it unreliable; it is no longer the preferred agent.
Erythromycin is the preferred alternative for pregnant women and children, not non-pregnant adults where ciprofloxacin is first-line.
clinicalPearl: Single-dose ciprofloxacin 1 g is the WHO first-line antibiotic for cholera in non-pregnant adults; erythromycin is reserved for pregnant women and children.
keyClue: Non-pregnant adult, confirmed cholera outbreak, antibiotic adjunct needed.
commonExamTrap: Selecting erythromycin (which is the alternative for pregnant women/children) or co-trimoxazole (now largely resistant).
missedConcept: Antibiotic selection in cholera depends on patient population — ciprofloxacin for adults, erythromycin for pregnant women and children.

clinicalStem: A primary care physician in a coastal district confirms a case of cholera in a 42-year-old fisherman who consumed untreated well water. Three of his household members have loose stools. The local water supply is suspected to be contaminated. Beyond treating the index case, which of the following public health actions is the MOST critical immediate priority?
options: Vaccinate the entire district population with oral cholera vaccine immediately
Prescribe prophylactic doxycycline to all household contacts
Notify public health authorities and implement safe water and sanitation measures
Arrange stool cultures for all household contacts before any intervention
Advise the household to boil water and take no further action pending lab confirmation
explanation: Cholera is a notifiable disease and the immediate public health priority is reporting to authorities to trigger a coordinated outbreak response, while simultaneously implementing safe water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) measures to interrupt transmission. Water contamination control is the cornerstone of cholera prevention at community level.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral cholera vaccine plays a role in outbreak control but requires lead time; immediate WASH and notification are the primary emergency responses.
Antibiotic prophylaxis for contacts is not standard WHO policy and risks promoting resistance; WASH measures break the chain of transmission more effectively.
Correct. Immediate notification and WASH implementation are the cornerstones of outbreak response.
Waiting for lab confirmation before any public health action delays containment; cholera is a clinical/epidemiological diagnosis in an outbreak context.
Advising only the household to boil water is insufficient when there is a community water supply contamination — a broader public health response is required.
clinicalPearl: Cholera is a notifiable disease — immediate reporting to public health authorities and WASH measures are the most critical early outbreak response actions.
keyClue: Suspected contaminated community water supply with household contacts symptomatic — community outbreak requiring public health response.
commonExamTrap: Focusing only on individual treatment or antibiotic prophylaxis rather than the population-level WASH and notification response.
missedConcept: Primary care physicians have a mandatory public health reporting role in cholera, and water/sanitation control breaks transmission more effectively than antibiotics alone.

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old university student returns from a 3-week trip to rural South Asia with a 10-day history of gradually rising fever (now 39.8°C), headache, malaise, and constipation. Examination reveals relative bradycardia, a faint maculopapular rash on the trunk, and mild splenomegaly. Blood cultures are positive for Salmonella typhi sensitive to fluoroquinolones. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Oral amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily for 14 days
Oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 7–10 days
Intramuscular ceftriaxone 2 g daily for 5 days
Oral azithromycin 500 mg daily for 7 days
Co-trimoxazole 960 mg twice daily for 14 days
explanation: For uncomplicated typhoid fever caused by fluoroquinolone-sensitive Salmonella typhi, oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 7–10 days is the WHO first-line treatment. It achieves excellent intracellular penetration and high cure rates. Fluoroquinolone sensitivity is confirmed by culture and sensitivity here.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Amoxicillin has been used historically but is now considered a second-line agent due to widespread resistance; ciprofloxacin is preferred when sensitivity is confirmed.
Correct. Oral ciprofloxacin for 7–10 days is first-line for fluoroquinolone-sensitive uncomplicated typhoid.
IV ceftriaxone is reserved for severe typhoid (altered consciousness, intestinal perforation) or fluoroquinolone-resistant cases — not first-line for uncomplicated disease.
Azithromycin is an alternative for nalidixic acid-resistant or multidrug-resistant typhoid, not first-line when fluoroquinolone sensitivity is confirmed.
Co-trimoxazole has widespread resistance in Salmonella typhi and is no longer a reliable first-line agent.
clinicalPearl: Ciprofloxacin is the first-line drug for uncomplicated fluoroquinolone-sensitive typhoid; switch to azithromycin or ceftriaxone if resistance is detected.
keyClue: Blood culture positive for S. typhi sensitive to fluoroquinolones — guides antibiotic choice directly.
commonExamTrap: Choosing azithromycin (appropriate for resistant strains) or ceftriaxone (for severe disease) when the sensitivity profile allows oral ciprofloxacin.
missedConcept: Antibiotic selection in typhoid must be guided by susceptibility testing — fluoroquinolone sensitivity makes ciprofloxacin the optimal choice.

clinicalStem: A 22-year-old male with a 2-week history of untreated fever and abdominal discomfort suddenly develops severe diffuse abdominal pain. On examination: temperature 38.4°C, pulse 128 bpm, BP 90/60 mmHg, rigid abdomen with generalised guarding and rebound tenderness. Plain abdominal X-ray shows free air under the diaphragm. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Start IV ciprofloxacin and observe for 24 hours before any surgical decision
Urgent surgical referral for exploratory laparotomy, combined with IV fluid resuscitation and broad-spectrum antibiotics
Nasogastric tube insertion and oral ciprofloxacin therapy
IV dexamethasone to reduce intestinal inflammation and perforation sealing
Repeat abdominal X-ray in 6 hours to confirm perforation before surgery
explanation: Free air under the diaphragm with peritonitis and shock in a patient with untreated typhoid fever indicates intestinal perforation, the most feared complication of typhoid. This is a surgical emergency requiring urgent laparotomy, combined with aggressive IV fluid resuscitation and broad-spectrum antibiotics covering Gram-negative organisms and anaerobes. Any delay significantly increases mortality.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Antibiotics alone cannot seal a perforated bowel; observing without surgical intervention in the setting of peritonitis and shock is dangerous and incorrect.
Correct. Intestinal perforation with peritonitis and shock is a surgical emergency requiring immediate laparotomy, resuscitation, and antibiotics.
An NGT may be placed perioperatively, but oral antibiotics and tube alone are wholly inadequate for a perforated viscus with peritonitis.
Corticosteroids have no role in typhoid perforation management; they do not seal perforations and may mask deterioration.
Free air under the diaphragm with clinical peritonitis is sufficient to diagnose perforation — delaying surgery for repeat imaging wastes critical time.
clinicalPearl: Intestinal perforation is the most lethal complication of typhoid — free air plus peritonitis mandates emergency laparotomy; do not delay for additional imaging.
keyClue: Free air under diaphragm + peritonitis + shock in context of 2-week untreated fever — typhoid perforation requiring emergency surgery.
commonExamTrap: Choosing antibiotics alone or deferring surgery pending further investigations, missing that this is a time-critical surgical emergency.
missedConcept: Typhoid intestinal perforation is a surgical emergency — medical management alone is insufficient once perforation has occurred.
redFlagReason: Intestinal perforation with peritonitis and haemodynamic shock — immediate surgical intervention is life-saving; delay is fatal.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old female food handler has repeated positive stool cultures for Salmonella typhi over 12 months despite feeling well. She has no current symptoms. Abdominal ultrasound shows gallstones. She has not received antibiotics for this finding. What is the most appropriate management?
options: No treatment needed as she is asymptomatic
Prolonged course of oral ciprofloxacin (4 weeks) with consideration of cholecystectomy
Single-dose azithromycin and repeat stool culture in 1 week
Oral amoxicillin for 6 weeks and stool surveillance
Typhoid vaccination and removal from food handling pending culture clearance
explanation: Chronic typhoid carriers (positive stool cultures >12 months) frequently harbour Salmonella typhi in the gallbladder, particularly when gallstones are present. Management involves a prolonged course of ciprofloxacin (or ampicillin in some guidelines) for 4 weeks, and cholecystectomy is recommended when gallstones are the reservoir. Without eradication she poses a significant public health risk as a food handler.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Doing nothing is inappropriate — a chronic carrier who is a food handler poses a major public health risk and requires active treatment.
Correct. Prolonged ciprofloxacin with cholecystectomy (given gallstones as the likely reservoir) is the appropriate strategy for chronic gallbladder carriage.
Single-dose azithromycin is insufficient for a chronic carrier with a gallbladder reservoir; prolonged therapy is required.
Amoxicillin is not the preferred agent for fluoroquinolone-susceptible chronic carriers; ciprofloxacin achieves better bile penetration.
While removing the carrier from food handling is essential, it is an adjunct, not the primary treatment — active eradication must be pursued.
clinicalPearl: Chronic typhoid carriers with gallstones require prolonged ciprofloxacin plus cholecystectomy — the gallbladder is the reservoir and surgery prevents relapse.
keyClue: Positive stool cultures >12 months + gallstones in a food handler = chronic gallbladder carrier state.
commonExamTrap: Ignoring the carrier state because the patient is asymptomatic, or failing to recommend cholecystectomy when gallstones are present.
missedConcept: Gallstones are the key risk factor for chronic typhoid carriage; cholecystectomy is part of definitive management.

clinicalStem: A 26-year-old unvaccinated medical student sustains a needlestick injury from a hollow-bore needle used on a patient known to be HBsAg-positive and HBeAg-positive. The incident occurred 3 hours ago. He presents to the occupational health clinic. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Start the full 3-dose hepatitis B vaccine series today and follow up
Administer hepatitis B immunoglobulin (HBIG) alone; vaccine is unnecessary if HBIG is given
Administer HBIG within 24 hours AND begin the hepatitis B vaccine series immediately
Await HBsAb titres from the source patient before deciding on prophylaxis
Prescribe oral tenofovir as post-exposure antiviral prophylaxis
explanation: For a needlestick from a known HBsAg-positive (especially HBeAg-positive, indicating high infectivity) source in an unvaccinated person, immediate post-exposure prophylaxis requires BOTH HBIG (passive immunity, within 24–48 hours ideally) AND simultaneous initiation of the hepatitis B vaccine series (active immunity). This combination provides the highest protection against HBV transmission.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Vaccine alone without HBIG is insufficient for high-risk exposure to a known HBsAg-positive source — passive immunity is needed urgently while active immunity develops.
HBIG alone provides only temporary passive protection; the vaccine series must also be started to achieve long-term active immunity.
Correct. Combined HBIG + hepatitis B vaccine is the standard of care for unvaccinated individuals after high-risk HBV exposure.
Waiting for source serologies is unnecessary when the source is already confirmed HBsAg-positive — every hour's delay reduces prophylaxis efficacy.
There is no approved oral antiviral post-exposure prophylaxis protocol for HBV in occupational settings; HBIG + vaccine is the evidence-based approach.
clinicalPearl: Unvaccinated healthcare workers exposed to HBsAg-positive blood need HBIG + hepatitis B vaccine immediately — combined passive-active prophylaxis is most effective.
keyClue: Unvaccinated + needlestick from HBsAg+/HBeAg+ source = highest-risk HBV exposure requiring dual prophylaxis.
commonExamTrap: Giving vaccine alone or HBIG alone instead of the mandatory combination for high-risk exposure.
missedConcept: HBIG provides immediate passive protection while the vaccine series builds active immunity — both are required after high-risk HBV exposure in an unvaccinated person.
redFlagReason: Time-critical exposure to highly infectious blood — delayed or incomplete prophylaxis can result in HBV infection; HBIG must be given within 24 hours.

subtopic: Supportive care and indications for referral
clinicalStem: A 17-year-old male presents with a 1-week history of fatigue, anorexia, nausea, and jaundice after eating at a street food stall. Examination shows scleral icterus and mild tender hepatomegaly. LFTs: ALT 1820 U/L, bilirubin 85 µmol/L. Anti-HAV IgM is positive. He is alert, oriented, and able to eat small amounts. Prothrombin time is normal. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Admit for IV antiviral therapy with ribavirin
Prescribe oral prednisolone to reduce hepatic inflammation
Advise rest, adequate nutrition, abstain from alcohol, and avoid hepatotoxic drugs; outpatient follow-up
Refer urgently for liver transplant assessment
Start oral tenofovir disoproxil fumarate
explanation: Acute hepatitis A is a self-limiting illness in immunocompetent patients. Management is supportive: rest, adequate nutrition, hydration, alcohol avoidance, and stopping hepatotoxic drugs. Outpatient management is appropriate when the patient is alert, able to eat, and prothrombin time is normal (no signs of fulminant hepatic failure). No specific antiviral therapy exists for hepatitis A.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Ribavirin has no proven benefit in hepatitis A; there is no approved antiviral for HAV infection.
Corticosteroids are not indicated in uncomplicated acute viral hepatitis and may worsen outcomes.
Correct. Supportive outpatient care is appropriate for uncomplicated acute hepatitis A with preserved synthetic function.
Liver transplant assessment is only relevant if the patient develops fulminant hepatic failure (encephalopathy, coagulopathy) — this patient has normal PT and is alert.
Tenofovir is an antiviral for hepatitis B and HIV, not hepatitis A.
clinicalPearl: Hepatitis A is self-limiting — treatment is supportive; refer urgently only if signs of fulminant hepatic failure (encephalopathy, coagulopathy) develop.
keyClue: Anti-HAV IgM positive + normal PT + alert and eating = uncomplicated acute hepatitis A suitable for outpatient supportive care.
commonExamTrap: Over-treating with antivirals or steroids, or referring for transplant assessment when the patient shows no signs of hepatic failure.
missedConcept: Normal prothrombin time and preserved consciousness rule out fulminant hepatic failure — uncomplicated hepatitis A requires only supportive care.

clinicalStem: A 48-year-old male who injected drugs in the 1990s attends for a routine health check. He feels well, has no symptoms, and denies current drug use. His liver function tests are mildly elevated (ALT 68 U/L). Which initial test is most appropriate to screen for hepatitis C?
options: HCV RNA PCR (quantitative)
Anti-HCV antibody test
HCV genotype testing
Liver biopsy
HBsAg and anti-HBs titres
explanation: Anti-HCV antibody testing is the recommended first-line screening test for hepatitis C. It detects past or current exposure. If positive, a confirmatory HCV RNA PCR is performed to establish active infection. The patient has a significant risk factor (previous IV drug use) making HCV screening clinically indicated.
wrongAnswerExplanations: HCV RNA PCR is the confirmatory test for active infection, used after a positive antibody screen — it is not the initial screening test.
Correct. Anti-HCV antibody is the WHO-recommended initial screening test; a positive result is followed by HCV RNA PCR.
Genotype testing is only relevant after active infection is confirmed by PCR; it guides treatment choice but has no role as a screening test.
Liver biopsy assesses fibrosis severity but is invasive; it is not a screening test and is only considered after confirming active infection.
HBsAg screening is for hepatitis B; this patient's risk factors point specifically to HCV screening.
clinicalPearl: HCV screening begins with an anti-HCV antibody test; a positive result must be confirmed with HCV RNA PCR to distinguish active from resolved infection.
keyClue: History of IV drug use in the 1990s + mildly elevated ALT = classic risk profile for occult HCV infection requiring antibody screening.
commonExamTrap: Ordering HCV RNA PCR first instead of the antibody screen, or ordering liver biopsy before confirming diagnosis.
missedConcept: The two-step HCV testing pathway: antibody screen first, then PCR confirmation — the antibody cannot distinguish active from resolved infection.

clinicalStem: A 29-year-old woman in her third trimester of pregnancy presents with a 5-day history of jaundice, right upper quadrant pain, and progressive confusion. She returned from a rural area with poor sanitation 3 weeks ago. Investigations: ALT 2900 U/L, total bilirubin 210 µmol/L, INR 3.8. Anti-HEV IgM is positive. Which statement about her prognosis is most accurate?
options: Hepatitis E in pregnancy follows the same benign course as in non-pregnant adults
Mortality from fulminant hepatic failure in pregnant women with HEV can reach 20–30%
The elevated INR is likely due to vitamin K deficiency from cholestasis, not liver failure
Fulminant HEV is only seen in the first trimester
Anti-HEV IgM positivity alone confirms fulminant hepatic failure
explanation: Hepatitis E has a disproportionately severe course in pregnant women, particularly in the third trimester, with reported mortality from fulminant hepatic failure of 20–30% — far higher than the <1% seen in non-pregnant adults. This patient's encephalopathy, markedly elevated bilirubin, and coagulopathy (INR 3.8) indicate fulminant hepatic failure requiring urgent specialist referral and monitoring.
wrongAnswerExplanations: HEV does NOT follow a benign course in pregnant women — the risk of fulminant hepatic failure is dramatically increased, especially in the third trimester.
Correct. HEV in pregnancy, particularly third trimester, carries a 20–30% mortality from fulminant hepatic failure — this is a well-documented and clinically critical fact.
An INR of 3.8 with encephalopathy represents severe hepatic synthetic failure, not simple vitamin K deficiency from cholestasis.
Fulminant HEV is most severe and common in the second and third trimesters, not the first trimester.
Anti-HEV IgM confirms acute HEV infection; the clinical diagnosis of fulminant hepatic failure is based on coagulopathy and encephalopathy, not serology alone.
clinicalPearl: Hepatitis E in the third trimester of pregnancy carries a 20–30% mortality risk from fulminant hepatic failure — urgently refer and monitor for encephalopathy and coagulopathy.
keyClue: Third trimester + jaundice + confusion + INR 3.8 + anti-HEV IgM positive = fulminant HEV in pregnancy with very high mortality.
commonExamTrap: Assuming HEV behaves the same in pregnant and non-pregnant patients, or attributing coagulopathy to vitamin K deficiency.
missedConcept: Hepatitis E is uniquely dangerous in pregnancy — the third trimester dramatically amplifies the risk of fulminant hepatic failure and death.
redFlagReason: Fulminant hepatic failure (encephalopathy + INR 3.8) in a third-trimester pregnant woman with HEV — 20–30% mortality risk requiring urgent specialist care.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old male sheep farmer presents with a 5-week history of undulant fever, drenching night sweats, arthralgia, and profound fatigue. He regularly consumes unpasteurised goat cheese. Examination shows mild splenomegaly. Brucella serology (SAT titre >1:160) and blood culture are positive for Brucella melitensis. Which antibiotic regimen is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 6 weeks PLUS rifampicin 600–900 mg daily for 6 weeks
Intramuscular streptomycin for 3 weeks monotherapy
Oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 4 weeks monotherapy
Oral co-trimoxazole 960 mg twice daily for 4 weeks monotherapy
IV ceftriaxone 2 g daily for 14 days monotherapy
explanation: WHO guidelines recommend combination antibiotic therapy for brucellosis to reduce relapse rates. The standard first-line regimen is doxycycline 100 mg twice daily plus rifampicin 600–900 mg daily, both for 6 weeks. Monotherapy has high relapse rates. Doxycycline + streptomycin (21 days IM) is an alternative with possibly lower relapse, preferred for complicated disease.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Doxycycline + rifampicin for 6 weeks is the WHO first-line combination regimen for uncomplicated brucellosis.
Streptomycin monotherapy has high relapse rates; it is used as part of combination therapy (with doxycycline) for complicated cases, not as monotherapy.
Fluoroquinolone monotherapy is associated with high relapse rates and is not recommended as first-line treatment for brucellosis.
Co-trimoxazole monotherapy has poor efficacy in brucellosis and high relapse rates; it may be used in special populations (e.g., children) but always in combination.
Ceftriaxone alone is inadequate for brucellosis; it may be part of triple therapy in neurobrucellosis but not standard monotherapy.
clinicalPearl: Brucellosis requires combination antibiotic therapy for 6 weeks — doxycycline + rifampicin is the WHO first-line regimen; monotherapy leads to unacceptably high relapse rates.
keyClue: Undulant fever + unpasteurised dairy/livestock exposure + positive serology = brucellosis requiring combination antibiotic treatment.
commonExamTrap: Selecting monotherapy (ciprofloxacin or co-trimoxazole alone) rather than mandatory combination therapy.
missedConcept: Brucellosis must always be treated with combination antibiotics for at least 6 weeks — monotherapy consistently results in high relapse rates.

clinicalStem: A 50-year-old male veterinarian with confirmed brucellosis, treated for 3 weeks with doxycycline and rifampicin, develops new-onset severe low back pain radiating to the right leg, with morning stiffness and point tenderness over L4–L5. MRI spine shows disc space narrowing and vertebral end-plate erosion at L4–L5. Which of the following is the most appropriate next management step?
options: Stop current antibiotics and switch to oral azithromycin monotherapy for 6 weeks
Urgent neurosurgical referral for spinal decompression as the primary treatment
Extend antibiotic therapy to at least 12 weeks, add streptomycin, and refer to a specialist for spinal brucellosis management
Prescribe NSAIDs for mechanical back pain and reassess in 6 weeks
Repeat MRI in 3 months before modifying antibiotic therapy
explanation: Vertebral osteomyelitis (spinal brucellosis/brucellar spondylitis) is a focal complication of brucellosis that requires prolonged combination antibiotic therapy — typically extended to at least 12 weeks. Adding an aminoglycoside (streptomycin or gentamicin) to the regimen is recommended for focal complications. Specialist referral is essential for monitoring and managing potential neurological compromise.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Switching to azithromycin monotherapy would be inadequate — focal complications of brucellosis require prolonged combination therapy, not a single less-effective agent.
Urgent surgical decompression is only indicated if there is progressive neurological deficit or spinal instability; the primary treatment is extended antibiotic combination therapy.
Correct. Spinal brucellosis requires extended combination antibiotic therapy (at least 12 weeks) with an aminoglycoside, plus specialist oversight.
This is a serious infectious complication requiring antibiotic modification — NSAID therapy alone would allow disease progression and potential spinal cord compromise.
Waiting 3 months without adjusting therapy would allow disease progression; the MRI findings already confirm the complication requiring immediate management change.
clinicalPearl: Brucellar spondylitis requires extended antibiotic therapy (at least 12 weeks) with a triple regimen including an aminoglycoside — the standard 6-week regimen is insufficient for focal complications.
keyClue: MRI showing disc space narrowing and vertebral end-plate erosion during brucellosis treatment = brucellar spondylitis requiring extended therapy.
commonExamTrap: Continuing the standard 6-week regimen without modification, or diagnosing mechanical back pain without considering focal infection.
missedConcept: Focal complications of brucellosis (spondylitis, neurobrucellosis, endocarditis) require prolonged combination therapy beyond the standard 6-week course.
